skills_tested: Distinguishing SCP vs. Permission Boundary vs. Config use cases
Differentiating CloudTrail (audit logging) from Config Rules (compliance state verification)

text: A large enterprise uses 	extbf{AWS Organizations} with a root management account and two 	extbf{Organizational Units (OUs)}: \texttt{Production} and \texttt{Development}. An 	extbf{SCP} attached to the \texttt{Production} OU explicitly denies the action \texttt{iam:CreateRole}. A 	extbf{Permission Boundary} is attached to a developer's 	extbf{IAM User} in an account under the \texttt{Production} OU; this boundary allows \texttt{iam:CreateRole}. The developer's 	extbf{IAM Identity Policy} also explicitly allows \texttt{iam:CreateRole}. The developer attempts to create a new 	extbf{IAM Role} in their 	extbf{AWS account}. What is the outcome, and which policy layer is the definitive reason?

options.A: The action is \textbf{denied} because the 	extbf{SCP} attached to the \texttt{Production} OU contains an explicit deny, and SCPs act as a ceiling that cannot be overridden by any 	extbf{Permission Boundary} or 	extbf{Identity Policy}.
options.B: The action is \textbf{allowed} because the 	extbf{Permission Boundary} explicitly permits \texttt{iam:CreateRole}, and Permission Boundaries take precedence over 	extbf{SCPs} when attached directly to a principal.
options.C: The action is \textbf{denied} because the 	extbf{Permission Boundary} does not grant permissions by itself — it only limits them — so without a resource-based policy also allowing the action, the effective permission is denied.
options.D: The action is \textbf{allowed} because the developer's 	extbf{IAM Identity Policy} contains an explicit allow and there is no explicit deny within the same account-level policy evaluation chain; SCPs are only evaluated at the management account boundary.

explanation:
\textbf{A is correct:} 	extbf{AWS IAM Policy Evaluation Logic} follows a strict order. \textbf{SCPs} applied via 	extbf{AWS Organizations} are evaluated \textit{before} any account-level policies. An explicit \textbf{deny} in an SCP is absolute — it cannot be overridden by a 	extbf{Permission Boundary}, an 	extbf{Identity Policy}, or a 	extbf{Resource-Based Policy} within the affected account. Because the \texttt{Production} OU's SCP explicitly denies \texttt{iam:CreateRole}, every principal in every account under that OU is denied this action regardless of any other policy granting it. SCPs define the \textit{maximum permissions} available to accounts in an OU; they do not grant permissions themselves but \textit{constrain} what can be allowed.

\textbf{B is incorrect:} 	extbf{Permission Boundaries} do not override SCPs. A Permission Boundary defines the maximum permissions an \textit{IAM principal} can use, but it still operates within the constraints set by SCPs. Even if a Permission Boundary allows an action, an SCP deny at the OU level blocks it.

\textbf{C is incorrect:} While it is true that Permission Boundaries do not grant permissions on their own (effective permissions require both the boundary and the identity policy to allow the action), this is not the \textit{definitive reason} for the denial in this scenario. The SCP explicit deny is the controlling factor and would block the action even if C's reasoning were resolved.

\textbf{D is incorrect:} SCPs are evaluated for \textit{all member accounts} in an OU, not just at the management account level. The management account itself is the only account that is not restricted by SCPs applied to the root or OUs it is a member of. The developer's account is a member account under the \texttt{Production} OU and is fully subject to that OU's SCP.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: IAM Policy Evaluation Logic – Allow/Deny, Permission Boundaries, SCPs

text:
A healthcare company operates a multi-account \textbf{AWS Organization}. An engineer in the \textit{Clinical} Organizational Unit (OU) attempts to call \textbf{kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion} on a \textbf{AWS KMS} customer-managed key. The following policies are in effect:\n
\begin{itemize}
\item An \textbf{SCP} attached to the \textit{Clinical} OU contains: \texttt{Effect: Deny, Action: kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion, Resource: *} with \textbf{no conditions}.
\item The engineer's \textbf{IAM Role} has an identity-based policy with \texttt{Effect: Allow, Action: kms:*, Resource: *}.
\item The engineer's \textbf{IAM Role} has a \textbf{Permission Boundary} that explicitly allows \textbf{kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion}.
\item The \textbf{KMS key policy} (a resource-based policy) grants the engineer's role \texttt{Effect: Allow, Action: kms:*, Resource: *}.
\end{itemize}

The engineer is acting from within the same AWS account that owns the KMS key. According to \textit{AWS IAM policy evaluation logic}, what is the outcome and the \textbf{PRIMARY} reason?

options.A: The action is \textbf{ALLOWED} because both the \textbf{Permission Boundary} and the \textbf{KMS key policy} explicitly permit \textbf{kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion}, and together they override the \textbf{SCP} Deny under the \textit{dual-allow} reconciliation rule.
options.B: The action is \textbf{DENIED} because the \textbf{Permission Boundary} is evaluated before the \textbf{KMS key policy}, and the boundary's intersection with the identity-based policy produces the effective permission set — the \textbf{SCP} is only consulted afterwards.
options.C: The action is \textbf{DENIED} because the \textbf{SCP} on the \textit{Clinical} OU contains an \textit{explicit Deny} for \textbf{kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion} with no conditions. An explicit Deny in any policy — evaluated simultaneously across all policy types — overrides all explicit Allows in identity-based, resource-based, and \textbf{Permission Boundary} policies, regardless of their contents.
options.D: The action is \textbf{ALLOWED} because the \textbf{KMS key policy} is a resource-based policy on a same-account resource, and same-account resource-based policy Allows are not subject to \textbf{SCP} restrictions — only cross-account access requires SCP authorization.

explanation:
extbf{C is correct:} Under AWS IAM policy evaluation logic, 	extbf{all policy types are evaluated together for explicit denies} — AWS does not sequentially gate on SCPs before examining other policies. The rule is: an 	extit{explicit Deny in any policy always overrides any explicit Allow, regardless of which policy type contains either statement}. The 	extbf{SCP} on the 	extit{Clinical} OU contains an unconditional explicit Deny for 	extbf{kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion}. Because an explicit Deny is present, the final result is 	extbf{DENY}. The SCP wins not because it is evaluated first in a strict sequence, but because 	extit{explicit Deny beats explicit Allow} — a fundamental, non-negotiable principle of AWS policy evaluation. The Permission Boundary allowing the action, the identity-based 	extbf{Allow kms:*}, and the KMS key policy 	extbf{Allow kms:*} are all irrelevant once an explicit Deny exists in any evaluated policy.

	extbf{A is incorrect:} No 	extit{dual-allow} reconciliation rule exists in AWS IAM. A Permission Boundary and a resource-based policy both allowing an action cannot together override an explicit Deny in an 	extbf{SCP}. Explicit Deny always wins, regardless of how many or which policy types contain Allows.

	extbf{B is incorrect:} This option incorrectly describes a strict sequential evaluation order where the 	extbf{Permission Boundary} is evaluated before the 	extbf{KMS key policy} and the SCP is consulted last. AWS does not process policy types in a fixed sequential gate order for the purpose of explicit deny checks. All policy types are evaluated together for explicit denies. The SCP explicit Deny produces the final outcome because explicit Deny overrides explicit Allow — not because of positional precedence.

	extbf{D is incorrect:} 	extbf{SCPs apply to all API calls made by principals in the affected account or OU, including calls to same-account resources.} There is no exception for same-account resource-based policies. SCPs constrain what principals within the OU are permitted to do; they do not distinguish between same-account and cross-account resource access. A same-account 	extbf{KMS key policy} granting an Allow does not exempt the principal from SCP restrictions.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: IAM Policy Evaluation Logic – Allow/Deny, Permission Boundaries, SCPs

text: A company is implementing \textbf{AWS Organizations} to manage 18 AWS accounts across multiple business units. The security team requires that all accounts prevent the deletion of \textbf{Amazon CloudTrail} logs and restrict the creation of public \textbf{Amazon S3} buckets. Which approach uses \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} most effectively to enforce these controls across the organization?

options.A: Apply an SCP at the \textbf{Organization Root} that denies \texttt{cloudtrail:DeleteTrail} and \texttt{s3:PutBucketAcl} actions for all principals
options.B: Apply separate SCPs at the \textbf{Organizational Unit (OU)} level that deny \texttt{cloudtrail:DeleteTrail} and require \textbf{Block Public Access} settings before bucket creation
options.C: Apply an SCP at the \textbf{Organization Root} that allows only \texttt{cloudtrail:PutTrail} and restricts \textbf{S3} bucket listing to prevent public access
options.D: Create individual \textbf{IAM policies} in each of the 18 accounts that deny deletion of CloudTrail logs and public \textbf{S3} bucket configuration

explanation:
\textbf{A is correct:} Service Control Policies (SCPs) are permission boundaries that restrict what actions are allowed across all accounts in an organization. Applying an SCP at the \textbf{Organization Root} with a \texttt{Deny} effect ensures universal enforcement across all 18 accounts. Denying \texttt{cloudtrail:DeleteTrail} prevents CloudTrail log deletion organization-wide. Denying \texttt{s3:PutBucketAcl} prevents granting public access via bucket ACLs, which is a primary mechanism for making \textbf{S3} buckets public. This is the most effective approach because: (1) SCPs operate at the account level and cannot be overridden by \textbf{IAM policies}, (2) root-level application ensures all accounts are protected regardless of OU structure, and (3) a single policy covers both requirements with clear, enforceable actions.

\textbf{B is incorrect:} Applying SCPs at the OU level rather than the \textbf{Organization Root} leaves other OUs potentially unprotected if they are not assigned the policy. Additionally, SCPs do not have a \textit{require} mechanism — they use \texttt{Deny} and \texttt{Allow} statements. An SCP cannot enforce that \textbf{Block Public Access} settings must be configured before bucket creation; it can only prevent or permit actions.

\textbf{C is incorrect:} Allowing only \texttt{cloudtrail:PutTrail} while denying deletion works in principle, but the S3 portion is flawed. Restricting bucket listing does not prevent public bucket creation — the \texttt{s3:ListBucket} action is for reading bucket contents, not controlling access permissions. An attacker with \texttt{s3:PutBucketAcl} or \texttt{s3:PutBucketPolicy} permissions could still make buckets public regardless of listing restrictions.

\textbf{D is incorrect:} \textbf{IAM policies} are account-specific and do not provide organization-wide governance. Individual policies would require deployment and maintenance across all 18 accounts and are difficult to enforce consistently. SCPs are the correct mechanism for cross-account, organization-wide enforcement at the boundary level.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: AWS Organizations – SCPs, OU Structure, Account Management

text: A company is implementing a multi-account strategy using \textbf{AWS Organizations}. The security team needs to ensure that all member accounts cannot use certain AWS services (such as \textbf{Amazon EC2} in unauthorized regions) and cannot disable \textbf{AWS CloudTrail} logging. Which approach BEST meets this requirement?

options.A: Apply an \textbf{IAM policy} to each individual user account to block unauthorized actions across all member accounts
options.B: Use \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} at the \textbf{Organization Root} or on specific \textbf{Organizational Units (OUs)} to enforce restrictions across all member accounts
options.C: Configure \textbf{AWS Config rules} on each member account to detect policy violations and send alerts to the security team
options.D: Deploy \textbf{AWS Systems Manager} to remotely disable restricted services on each member account instance

explanation:
\textbf{B is correct:} \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} are permission boundaries that apply to all \textbf{IAM principals} (users, roles) in member accounts. SCPs attached at the \textbf{Organization Root} or OU level act as a centralized governance layer, preventing member accounts from performing actions—regardless of their local \textbf{IAM permissions}. This is the standard AWS approach for enforcing organization-wide compliance controls like restricting regional access and preventing service disablement.

\textbf{A is incorrect:} \textbf{IAM policies} within individual accounts do not prevent a determined administrator from modifying those policies locally. \textbf{IAM} lacks cross-account enforcement by design.

\textbf{C is incorrect:} \textbf{AWS Config rules} are detective controls (they identify violations after the fact) rather than preventive controls. Config rules cannot block actions; they only monitor compliance.

\textbf{D is incorrect:} \textbf{AWS Systems Manager} is an operational tool for patch management and configuration management on instances—not a service-level governance mechanism. It cannot prevent account administrators from enabling restricted services.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: AWS Organizations – SCPs, OU Structure, Account Management

text: A solutions architect is designing a governance framework for a multi-account AWS organization with 34 development and production accounts. The organization requires that no \textbf{Amazon RDS} database can be launched without encryption enabled, and that developers in non-production accounts must be restricted from launching \textbf{EC2} instances larger than \textbf{t3.large}. Additionally, all AWS API calls must be logged for audit compliance. Which combination of AWS Organizations features and strategies would \textbf{BEST} enforce these policies with the \textbf{LEAST operational overhead}?

options.A: Create separate \textbf{Organizational Units (OUs)} for development and production accounts. Attach \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} to deny \textbf{RDS} launch without encryption and to deny \textbf{EC2} instance types larger than \textbf{t3.large} in the development OU. Enable \textbf{AWS CloudTrail} at the organization level.
options.B: Deploy individual \textbf{IAM policies} to each of the 34 accounts to restrict resource launches. Use \textbf{AWS Config} rules in each account to monitor compliance and trigger remediation \textbf{Lambda} functions.
options.C: Attach permissive \textbf{SCPs} to all accounts and rely on \textbf{AWS CloudTrail} logging combined with manual quarterly audits to identify and manually remediate non-compliant resource launches.
options.D: Implement \textbf{AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM)} to share a centralized template library across all accounts, then use \textbf{AWS CloudFormation StackSets} to deploy only approved resources.

explanation:
\textbf{A is correct:} \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} are the foundational preventive governance tool for enforcing organization-wide policies across multiple accounts in \textbf{AWS Organizations}. By organizing accounts into \textbf{OUs} and attaching SCPs at the appropriate level, the architect can:

\textbullet{} Attach an SCP to the root or parent OU denying \textbf{rds:CreateDBInstance} actions where the \textit{StorageEncrypted} condition is not set to \textit{true}. This uses a valid condition key on the CreateDBInstance API action to enforce encryption at launch time.

\textbullet{} Attach a separate SCP to the development OU denying \textbf{ec2:RunInstances} for instance types larger than \textbf{t3.large} using the \textit{ec2:InstanceType} condition key.

\textbullet{} Enable \textbf{AWS CloudTrail} at the organization level to audit all API calls across all accounts—satisfying the logging requirement with centralized, automated collection.

This approach is \textit{preventive} (stops non-compliant actions before they occur), scales automatically to all 34 accounts, and requires minimal ongoing operational overhead once policies are deployed.

\textbf{B is incorrect:} Managing 34 separate \textbf{IAM policies} across individual accounts creates significant operational overhead, version control complexity, and inconsistency. Policies can be modified locally by account administrators, undermining organization-wide control. \textbf{AWS Config} remediation adds complexity and is reactive rather than preventive.

\textbf{C is incorrect:} Relying on \textbf{CloudTrail} logging alone without preventive SCPs is detective (reactive), not preventive. Quarterly manual audits are not real-time enforcement and allow non-compliant resources to be provisioned, incur costs, and create compliance gaps before detection occurs.

\textbf{D is incorrect:} \textbf{Resource Access Manager (RAM)} shares resources, not policies. \textbf{CloudFormation StackSets} deploy templates but do not prevent direct API calls to launch resources outside templates. Developers can still bypass approved templates and call AWS APIs directly, rendering this approach ineffective as a governance control.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: AWS Organizations – SCPs, OU Structure, Account Management

text: A company with 62 AWS accounts organized into three \textbf{Organizational Units (OUs)} is implementing security policies using \textbf{AWS Organizations}. The security team needs to prevent all accounts in the Development OU from launching instances larger than \texttt{t3.medium} and from modifying \textbf{Amazon S3} bucket policies. However, the Production OU must have unrestricted access to all instance types and full \textbf{S3} permissions. Which approach implements these requirements with the \textbf{LEAST operational overhead} and maintains the principle of \textit{least privilege}?

options.A: Create two \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} that explicitly allow only \texttt{t3.medium} instances and deny \textbf{S3} policy modifications, then attach them to individual Development accounts.
options.B: Create a single \textbf{SCP} that denies \texttt{EC2:RunInstances} with instance types larger than \texttt{t3.medium} and denies \textbf{s3:PutBucketPolicy}, attach it to the Development OU. The Production OU remains unaffected because no deny \textbf{SCP} is attached to it, and the default \textbf{FullAWSAccess SCP} continues to apply.
options.C: Attach \textbf{IAM permission boundaries} to all Development OU accounts to restrict instance types and \textbf{S3} bucket policy modifications, while applying no restrictions to Production accounts.
options.D: Use \textbf{AWS Config} rules to monitor and automatically remediate non-compliant \textbf{EC2} instances and \textbf{S3} bucket policy changes in the Development OU.

explanation:
\textbf{B is correct:} \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} are the appropriate mechanism at the \textbf{AWS Organizations} level to enforce guardrails across multiple accounts within an OU. By attaching a deny-based \textbf{SCP} to the Development OU that denies \texttt{EC2:RunInstances} with oversized instance types and denies \textbf{s3:PutBucketPolicy}, all accounts in that OU are restricted. The Production OU remains unaffected because no deny \textbf{SCP} is attached to it, and the default \textbf{FullAWSAccess SCP} continues to apply. This approach provides centralized, scalable governance without modifying individual account policies and aligns with \textit{least privilege} by explicitly restricting unauthorized actions at the organizational boundary.

\textbf{A is incorrect:} Attaching policies to individual accounts (62 in this case) bypasses the OU structure and introduces significant operational overhead. OU-level governance is far more efficient and maintainable than managing policies on each account separately. Additionally, \textbf{SCPs} work on a deny-based model; explicit allow statements in an \textbf{SCP} do not constrain permissions the way the question requires.

\textbf{C is incorrect:} \textbf{IAM permission boundaries} are applied per user or role within an account, not at the OU level. This would require configuration on every Development account and every user/role — far more overhead than OU-level \textbf{SCPs}. Additionally, \textbf{SCPs} are the standard \textbf{AWS Organizations} mechanism for cross-account restrictions and enforce controls that cannot be bypassed by any \textbf{IAM} policy.

\textbf{D is incorrect:} \textbf{AWS Config} is a compliance monitoring and remediation tool, not an enforcement mechanism. It detects non-compliance but does not prevent actions proactively. \textbf{SCPs} prevent violations before they occur, making them superior for security governance.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: AWS Organizations – SCPs, OU Structure, Account Management
metadata.learning_objective: Understand OU-level governance using SCPs vs. account-level and user-level policies; understand SCP evaluation logic and default FullAWSAccess SCP

text: A large enterprise has a multi-account 	extbf{AWS Organizations} structure with the following 	extbf{Organizational Unit (OU)} hierarchy: a Root, a 	extbf{Sandbox OU} for developer experimentation, a 	extbf{Production OU} for live workloads, and a 	extbf{Security OU} for centralized security tooling accounts. The security team wants to enforce the following rules simultaneously: (1) prevent any account in the 	extbf{Sandbox OU} from launching resources in any AWS Region outside 	exttt{us-east-1} and 	exttt{eu-west-1}; (2) ensure that the central 	extbf{Security OU} accounts are completely exempt from Region restrictions so they can operate globally; and (3) ensure the policy cannot be overridden by account-level administrators. Which combination of actions meets all three requirements?

options.A: Attach a 	extbf{Service Control Policy (SCP)} that denies all actions outside 	exttt{us-east-1} and 	exttt{eu-west-1} to the 	extbf{Root}. Use a 	extbf{resource-based policy} on the 	extbf{Security OU} accounts to allow global access. Disable the SCP inheritance for the 	extbf{Security OU}.
options.B: Attach a 	extbf{Service Control Policy (SCP)} that denies actions outside 	exttt{us-east-1} and 	exttt{eu-west-1} directly to the 	extbf{Sandbox OU} only. Attach no Region-restricting 	extbf{SCP} to the 	extbf{Security OU} or 	extbf{Production OU}. Ensure 	extbf{FullAWSAccess SCP} remains attached to the 	extbf{Root}.
options.C: Attach a 	extbf{Service Control Policy (SCP)} that denies actions outside 	exttt{us-east-1} and 	exttt{eu-west-1} to the 	extbf{Root}. Add a second 	extbf{SCP} to the 	extbf{Security OU} that explicitly allows all Regions to override the Root SCP.
options.D: Create an 	extbf{IAM permission boundary} that restricts Regions to 	exttt{us-east-1} and 	exttt{eu-west-1} and attach it to all 	extbf{IAM roles} in every 	extbf{Sandbox OU} account. Grant 	extbf{Security OU} accounts 	exttt{AdministratorAccess} to bypass the boundary.

explanation:
extbf{B is correct:} 	extbf{SCPs} follow an 	extit{implicit deny} model — they restrict the maximum permissions available to accounts within the scope where they are attached. By attaching the Region-restricting 	extbf{SCP} 	extit{only} to the 	extbf{Sandbox OU}, the restriction applies exclusively to accounts within that OU. The 	extbf{Security OU} and 	extbf{Production OU} are unaffected because they do not inherit this SCP. The 	extbf{FullAWSAccess SCP} at the Root ensures all accounts retain their base permissions. Account-level administrators cannot override SCPs because SCPs are a management-plane guardrail enforced above the account level.

	extbf{A is incorrect:} 	extbf{SCPs} do not support 'disabling inheritance' for child OUs. There is no mechanism to disable SCP inheritance from the Root for a specific OU — an SCP attached to the Root applies to all accounts in the organization, including those in the 	extbf{Security OU}. 	extbf{Resource-based policies} cannot override SCPs.

	extbf{C is incorrect:} 	extbf{SCPs use a deny-overrides model} — an explicit 	exttt{Allow} in an SCP attached to a child OU cannot override an explicit 	exttt{Deny} in an SCP attached to the Root. The Security OU SCP would have no effect in overriding the Root-level deny.

	extbf{D is incorrect:} 	extbf{IAM permission boundaries} apply to individual 	extbf{IAM identities} (users and roles), not to entire accounts. They do not provide organization-wide guardrails, and granting 	exttt{AdministratorAccess} does not bypass a permission boundary — the boundary and the identity policy both apply using an intersection logic.

metadata.subject: Domain 1 – Design Secure Architectures
metadata.sub_topic: AWS Organizations – SCPs, OU Structure, Account Management

text:
A multinational financial services company is consolidating 12 AWS accounts across three business units into a single \textbf{AWS Organization}. The Chief Security Officer mandates that:

(1) Development accounts must never provision \textbf{Amazon RDS} instances outside the eu-west-1 region.
(2) Production accounts must restrict all EC2 instance types to graviton-based instances (t4g, c7g, m7g) for cost optimisation.
(3) Finance team members must be unable to delete \textbf{AWS CloudTrail} logs, even if they have full administrative permissions.
(4) Each business unit should manage its own \textbf{IAM} policies and user provisioning independently within its accounts.

The solutions architect is designing the \textbf{Organizational Unit (OU)} structure and control policies. Which combination of strategies BEST addresses all four requirements while minimising administrative overhead?

options.A: Create three separate OUs (one per business unit), apply \textbf{Service Control Policies (SCPs)} to the root OU to enforce region and instance type restrictions, assign an \textbf{IAM policy} in each OU to prevent CloudTrail deletion, and ensure each OU has separate delegated account management.
options.B: Create three business unit OUs and one shared services OU. Use \textbf{SCPs} to deny RDS provisioning outside eu-west-1 and restrict EC2 instance types. Create a separate \textquotedblleft Security\textquotedblright{} OU with an \textbf{AWS Config} rule to audit CloudTrail deletion attempts. Enable \textbf{IAM Identity Center} permission sets for each business unit.
options.C: Create three business unit OUs and one shared services OU. Apply \textbf{SCPs} denying RDS provisioning outside eu-west-1 and restricting non-graviton EC2 instances. Attach an \textbf{SCP} that denies \textbf{cloudtrail:DeleteTrail} and related actions to all accounts. Within each business unit OU, allow accounts to manage \textbf{IAM} independently by not restricting \textbf{IAM} actions via \textbf{SCP}.
options.D: Flatten the structure into individual accounts with no OUs. Use \textbf{IAM} permission boundaries in each account to enforce all four restrictions. Provision a centralised \textbf{AWS CloudFormation} template to distribute policies across all 12 accounts monthly.

explanation:
\textbf{C is correct:}

\textbf{SCPs} are the correct preventive mechanism for organisation-wide restrictions:
• \textbf{SCP} denying \textbf{ec2:RunInstances} with conditions restricting instance types to graviton-based instances enforces requirement (2).
• \textbf{SCP} denying \textbf{rds:CreateDBInstance} outside eu-west-1 enforces requirement (1).
• \textbf{SCP} denying \textbf{cloudtrail:DeleteTrail}, \textbf{cloudtrail:StopLogging}, and related actions applies across all accounts regardless of \textbf{IAM} permissions, satisfying requirement (3) — \textit{preventive controls cannot be overridden by account-level IAM policies}.
• Requirement (4) is satisfied by not applying \textbf{SCPs} that restrict \textbf{IAM} actions within business unit OUs, allowing each account to manage its own \textbf{IAM} users and roles independently.
• This approach scales with minimal ongoing overhead — \textbf{SCPs} are defined once and inherited by all child OUs and accounts.

\textbf{A is incorrect:}
• \textbf{IAM policies} cannot be attached to \textbf{Organizational Units}. Only \textbf{SCPs} and tag policies can be attached at the Organization level.
• While \textbf{SCPs} at the root OU would enforce restrictions, attempting to assign \textbf{IAM} policies to OUs is not valid AWS architecture.
• Does not properly prevent CloudTrail deletion across all accounts via a preventive control.

\textbf{B is incorrect:}
• \textbf{AWS Config} rules are \textit{detective controls} that audit and report compliance violations but do not prevent actions from occurring.
• Requirement (3) explicitly requires prevention (\textquotedblleft must be unable to\textquotedblright), not detection — CloudTrail deletion could succeed before \textbf{Config} detects it.
• \textbf{IAM Identity Center} permission sets are designed for federated user access and workforce identity management, not for segregating account-level \textbf{IAM} administration by business unit.
• Does not satisfy the preventive requirement for CloudTrail protection.

\textbf{D is incorrect:}
• Flat structure with no OUs eliminates the hierarchical governance model and scalability benefits of \textbf{AWS Organizations}.
• \textbf{IAM} permission boundaries operate only within a single account and cannot enforce organisation-wide restrictions; they would require manual distribution to all 12 accounts.
• Monthly \textbf{CloudFormation} updates introduce operational overhead and configuration drift risk.
• \textbf{IAM} permission boundaries cannot deny cross-account principals — they cannot satisfy requirement (3) for finance team members across multiple accounts.
